title: A Spatially-Coherent Attribution Framework for Interpreting Black-Box Tropical Cyclone Intensity Forecasts

research.question: Can a gradient-based attribution framework preserve geographic coherence while separating location effects from physical-feature interactions in black-box tropical-cyclone intensity forecasts?
research.system: Six tree-ensemble models forecast 24-hour tropical-cyclone intensity change in the Western North Pacific from tabular SHIPS/STIPS atmospheric, oceanic, and geographic predictors.
research.inputData: 6,380 training instances and 1,595 test instances with 46 predictors, including LAT, LON, vertical-wind-shear, sea-surface-temperature, ocean-heat-content, and potential-intensity variables; Typhoon Goni (2015) supplies the representative track analysis.
research.method: Geo-dSHAP estimates model gradients with Natural Evolution Strategies, derives covariance-based feature attribution, treats LAT and LON as one GEO player, maps location-feature interaction magnitudes with copula geostatistics, and stratifies attributions by storm lifecycle and intensity.
research.benchmark: The study compares XGB, LGBM, RF, HGB, GB, and ET by test MAE and R2, then compares their spatial main-effect maps with pattern-correlation coefficients using XGB as the reference.
research.uncertainty: Gradient estimates use a perturbation scale of 0.02, batches of 32 perturbations, and 100 Monte Carlo iterations for stability; the paper does not report predictive confidence intervals for the attribution maps.
research.conclusion: Models with similar forecast accuracy can encode sharply different spatial logic; for the selected XGB model, Geo-dSHAP exposes spatially and phase-dependent thermodynamic and shear sensitivities that accuracy metrics alone cannot diagnose.

narrative.throughline: A forecast can be accurate for the wrong spatial reasons, so geographic sensitivity must be separated from physical-feature sensitivity before the model can be trusted operationally.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that tropical-cyclone intensity depends on environmental predictors and that ensemble models can be accurate without revealing their reasoning.
narrative.stakes: Operational forecasters may accept a high-scoring model whose learned geographic dependencies conflict with physical expectations or fail outside the conditions represented in training.
narrative.resolution: Geo-dSHAP turns the forecast into a storm-track-centered field of location and feature sensitivities, revealing where and when the model relies on thermodynamic or shear information.

background.premise: Machine-learning models are increasingly used for tropical-cyclone intensity forecasting, but their black-box nature limits operational adoption.
background.visibleEvidence: A 24-hour tropical-cyclone intensity-change prediction emerges from an opaque machine-learning model.
background.whyNow: The attribution problem begins with the paper's stated operational-adoption limit for black-box forecasts.
background.boundary: The paper focuses on 24-hour intensity change in the Western North Pacific rather than all basins and forecast horizons.
background.sourceExcerpt: Machine learning (ML) models are increasingly used for Tropical Cyclone (TC) intensity forecasting. However, their black-box nature limits operational adoption.
background.visualObject: Opaque ensemble model outputting a 24-hour Western North Pacific intensity-change forecast
background.motionPurpose: Keep the model interior hidden while exposing only its forecast output.
background.stepDetail: Feed the predictor table into an opaque model box and reveal its 24-hour intensity-change prediction.
background.transitionBridge: The forecast marker becomes the output of an otherwise opaque ensemble model.

background.premise: Generic SHAP applied to high-dimensional geophysical data can fragment spatial coherence by treating grid locations independently.
background.visibleEvidence: One coherent warm-ocean patch breaks into independently permuted pixels and a noisy attribution mosaic.
background.whyNow: The viewer needs to see why an ordinary feature-attribution map can destroy the geographic object it is supposed to explain.
background.boundary: The criticism concerns generic independent-feature treatment of spatially structured geophysical inputs, not every possible SHAP implementation.
background.sourceExcerpt: yet standard approaches such as SHAP often disrupt the spatial coherence fundamental to geophysical data.
background.visualObject: Coherent geophysical patch transforming into a fragmented pixel-attribution field
background.motionPurpose: Separate neighboring cells to reveal the loss of relative location and spatial coherence.
background.stepDetail: Show a coherent field; permute cells independently; compare the intact and fragmented explanations.
background.transitionBridge: The fragmented cells regroup into a single geographic player.

background.premise: GeoShapley preserves a geographic player but high-resolution spatial attribution remains computationally demanding under combinatorial sampling.
background.visibleEvidence: A growing coalition tree fans into many model evaluations while a high-resolution map waits to be filled.
background.whyNow: Spatial coherence alone does not make the explanation operationally practical at forecast scale.
background.boundary: Paired sampling improves convergence, but the paper's motivation is the remaining cost of sampling-based estimation.
background.sourceExcerpt: but its sampling-based estimator is computationally expensive and, as shown here, fails to isolate the main spatial effect.
background.visualObject: Combinatorial coalition tree beside an unfinished attribution map
background.motionPurpose: Multiply coalition branches until their evaluation burden visibly overwhelms the map.
background.stepDetail: Start with one GEO player; add feature coalitions; count model calls; freeze the map before completion.
background.transitionBridge: The stalled sampling tree collapses into directional gradient probes.

methods.input: SHIPS/STIPS point-in-time atmospheric, oceanic, LAT, and LON predictors
methods.output: Six fitted ensemble regression models for 24-hour tropical-cyclone intensity change
methods.validity: Tree ensembles represent correlated nonlinear tabular predictors and are compared on the same training and test sets.
methods.sourceExcerpt: We apply Geo-dSHAP to six ensemble models. XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, HistGradientBoosting, Gradient Boosting, and ExtraTrees, trained to predict 24-hour TC intensity change in the Western North Pacific.
methods.visualObject: Six parallel ensemble forests receiving one common predictor vector
methods.motionPurpose: Duplicate the same predictor vector across models so later spatial differences cannot be attributed to different inputs.
methods.stepDetail: Assemble 46 predictors; split training and test instances; pass identical feature vectors through six ensemble regressors.
methods.transitionBridge: Each fitted model retains LAT and LON for joint treatment in M02.

methods.input: Separate LAT and LON feature coordinates
methods.validity: Joint treatment preserves the paired coordinates that define location-specific climatology and spatial memory.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, paragraph following Equation 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: i treat the geographic features (LAT, LON) as a single
methods.visualObject: LAT and LON axes folding into one map pin labeled GEO
methods.motionPurpose: Join the two coordinate axes into one indivisible spatial object.
methods.stepDetail: Highlight LAT; highlight LON; bracket both coordinates; replace the bracket with one GEO player.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted model and coherent GEO player receive standardized random perturbations in M03.

methods.input: A fitted black-box model and an isotropic Gaussian perturbation distribution
methods.output: Batches of standardized random directions for NES finite differences
methods.validity: The NES estimator evaluates small standardized Gaussian random perturbations; the reported application uses scale 0.02 and batches of 32.
methods.locator: PDF pages 3-4, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation and Section 3.2 Cross-model comparison
methods.visualObject: Thirty-two standardized direction arrows radiating from one predictor vector
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the model evaluations that replace unavailable exact tree gradients.
methods.stepDetail: Center a predictor vector, sample 32 Gaussian directions at scale 0.02, and pair their finite-difference probes.
methods.transitionBridge: The sampled probes are first evaluated on training-background instances in M04.

methods.input: NES perturbation batches and training-background instances
methods.output: Background gradient matrix Gbg with B rows and Nfeatures columns
methods.validity: The first Geo-dSHAP phase draws its background batch from training data and estimates one gradient row per background instance.
methods.sourceExcerpt: First, we estimate gradients on a background batch
methods.visualObject: Training-background rows accumulating directional probes into matrix Gbg
methods.motionPurpose: Own the training-background NES phase separately from local geographic evaluation.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate each perturbation batch on the training-background instances and place the estimated gradients into Gbg.
methods.transitionBridge: The background matrix supplies the global covariance calculation in M05.

methods.validity: The source defines C as the expectation of gradient outer products over the background dataset.
methods.visualObject: Stacked background gradients condensing into a square covariance heatmap
methods.motionPurpose: Fold repeated gradient vectors into one matrix that exposes diagonal importance and cross-feature structure.
methods.stepDetail: Arrange background gradients as rows; pair gradient components; average their products; reveal matrix C.
methods.transitionBridge: A separate NES phase now evaluates fixed test-set geographic locations in M06.

methods.input: NES perturbation batches and fixed test-set geographic locations Xgeo
methods.output: Local gradient matrix Ggeo with S rows and Nfeatures columns
methods.validity: The second Geo-dSHAP phase applies the same NES estimator to a fixed set of test-set geographic locations.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Second, we apply the same NES estimator to a fixed set
methods.visualObject: Fixed test locations receiving the same directional probes and filling matrix Ggeo
methods.motionPurpose: Own the local test-location NES phase separately from the training-background phase.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the test locations fixed, repeat the NES probes at each location, and store the local gradients in Ggeo.
methods.transitionBridge: Repeated background and test estimates are combined across Monte Carlo iterations in M07.

methods.input: Repeated background and test gradient estimates
methods.output: Monte Carlo averaged gradient estimates with iteration-to-iteration spread
methods.validity: The application averages results over 100 Monte Carlo iterations to support stability while the method states that repeated iterations quantify estimate uncertainty.
methods.sourceExcerpt: averaged the results over 100 Monte Carlo iterations
methods.visualObject: One hundred gradient estimates forming distributions that collapse to mean markers with visible spread
methods.motionPurpose: Expose both the averaged estimate and its Monte Carlo stability shape.
methods.stepDetail: Stack 100 iteration results, mark their spread, and average each corresponding gradient component.
methods.transitionBridge: The stabilized covariance entries become global feature attributions in M08.

methods.output: One global DerSHAP attribution value phi_i for each feature
methods.validity: The attribution sums the covariance diagonal term and half the absolute off-diagonal covariance contributions for the feature.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, equation following the covariance definition
methods.visualObject: Covariance matrix row resolving into one feature-attribution bar
methods.motionPurpose: Convert covariance structure into the global attribution assigned to each feature.
methods.stepDetail: Collect one diagonal cell with half of the absolute cross-feature cells into a single attribution bar.
methods.transitionBridge: The stabilized LAT and LON components are isolated for the geographic main effect in M09.

methods.input: LAT and LON gradient components at a geographic sample
methods.validity: The squared L2 norm isolates sensitivity to the joint geographic coordinates independently of physical-feature gradients.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, Equation 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: main effect of location, ϕgeo(x), is then quantified as the
methods.visualObject: Orthogonal LAT and LON arrows closing into one radial magnitude ring
methods.motionPurpose: Combine coordinate-direction sensitivity into one rotation-invariant location magnitude.
methods.stepDetail: Show LAT gradient; show LON gradient; square both lengths; combine them as one geographic magnitude.
methods.transitionBridge: The geographic magnitude is paired with one physical-feature gradient in M10.

methods.input: Geographic gradient magnitude and one physical-feature gradient magnitude
methods.validity: The proxy is large only where the prediction is sensitive to both location and the selected physical feature.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: proxy identifies regions where the model prediction is highly sensitive
methods.visualObject: GEO magnitude ring intersecting a POT or shear gradient bar
methods.motionPurpose: Make the interaction brighten only where both component sensitivities are present.
methods.stepDetail: Hold GEO magnitude fixed; introduce one feature gradient; multiply their lengths; encode the product as brightness.
methods.transitionBridge: The discrete interaction markers supply the empirical dependence needed to fit variogram parameters in M11.

methods.input: Sampled interaction values and a theoretical variogram model
methods.output: Optimized nugget sill and effective range for Copula interpolation
methods.validity: The source automatically optimizes nugget sill and effective range with Levenberg-Marquardt through scikit-gstat.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, final paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: hyperparameters (i.e., nugget, sill, and effective range) were
methods.visualObject: Empirical dependence points receiving a fitted theoretical variogram curve with three parameter markers
methods.motionPurpose: Own the parameter estimation that controls the regular-grid interpolation.
methods.stepDetail: Move nugget sill and effective-range controls until the Levenberg-Marquardt variogram fit settles on the empirical dependence points.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted variogram parameters join the discrete interaction values for interpolation in M12.

methods.input: Discrete interaction values with fitted nugget sill and effective range
methods.validity: Copula geostatistics uses rank-based transformations to preserve spatial dependence while accommodating non-Gaussian interaction magnitudes.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Gradient estimation, paragraph following Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: computed at the S sample points in Xgeo, are subsequently
methods.visualObject: Sparse colored samples expanding into a continuous basin-wide attribution surface
methods.motionPurpose: Grow a continuous spatial field from sampled interaction points without replacing the storm-track geometry.
methods.stepDetail: Plot sample points, rank-transform interaction values, and fill the regular grid with the fitted spatial dependence.
methods.transitionBridge: The completed spatial maps remain fixed while forecast models are scored against held-out observations in M13.

methods.input: Held-out intensity-change predictions and observations for each ensemble model
methods.output: One held-out mean absolute error for each of the six models
methods.validity: Section 3.1 defines MAE as average absolute prediction-observation deviation and reports train and test scores separately in Table 1.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Model performances, Table 1
methods.visualObject: Prediction-observation distances collapsing into six test-error bars
methods.motionPurpose: Own the held-out error calculation before it is used for model selection.
methods.stepDetail: Measure every held-out absolute prediction error and average the distances separately for each model.
methods.transitionBridge: The six test MAE values determine the reference model in M14.

methods.input: Test MAE values from the six ensemble models
methods.output: XGB reference model for detailed attribution
methods.validity: All models are evaluated on the same test dataset and XGB has the lowest reported test MAE.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Model performances, Table 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: MAE and was therefore adopted as the reference model for
methods.visualObject: Six MAE markers with XGB settling at the minimum
methods.motionPurpose: Rank the models by one shared error axis and retain XGB for the physical-sensitivity analysis.
methods.stepDetail: Place six test-MAE values; order them; highlight the minimum; carry XGB's map forward.
methods.transitionBridge: The retained XGB map becomes the reference for cross-model spatial comparison in M15.

methods.input: Six model-specific GEO main-effect maps with XGB designated as the reference
methods.output: PCC evidence that similarly accurate ensembles encode divergent spatial logic
methods.validity: The paper compares each model's spatial attribution pattern with the XGB reference and reports the resulting Pattern Correlation Coefficients.
methods.visualObject: Six GEO maps aligned to XGB with one pattern-correlation marker beneath each alternative model
methods.motionPurpose: Show that predictive accuracy and learned spatial reasoning are separate evaluation axes.
methods.stepDetail: Hold XGB as the reference; align each alternative map; compute its pattern correlation; reveal the low and negative PCC markers.
methods.transitionBridge: The selected XGB attributions are aggregated across track records in M16.

methods.input: Instance-level XGB Geo-dSHAP attributions sampled along observed tropical-cyclone tracks
methods.output: Aggregated XGB attribution records prepared for physical stratification
methods.validity: Section 3.6 explicitly extends the single-track analysis with aggregated Geo-dSHAP attributions before applying lifecycle and intensity criteria.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.6 Stratified analysis by TC lifecycle and intensity
methods.visualObject: Instance-level attribution markers from observed tracks collecting into one analysis table
methods.motionPurpose: Own the aggregation required by the subsequent lifecycle and intensity partitions.
methods.stepDetail: Collect the source-reported track attribution records into the aggregated analysis set without changing their physical labels.
methods.transitionBridge: The aggregated records receive the lifecycle partition in M17.

methods.output: Genesis mature and decay attribution groups along the storm track
methods.validity: The lifecycle partition follows the three phases used for the POT analysis in Figure 5.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.6 Stratified analysis by TC lifecycle and intensity, Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: storm track, partitioned into Genesis, Mature, and Decay
methods.visualObject: Storm track separating into Genesis mature and decay segments
methods.motionPurpose: Partition the continuous track so lifecycle-dependent sensitivity becomes visible.
methods.stepDetail: Assign each aggregated track attribution to its Genesis mature or decay segment.
methods.transitionBridge: The same aggregated records receive an independent intensity partition in M18.

methods.input: Aggregated XGB Geo-dSHAP attributions with JTWC storm classifications
methods.output: JTWC intensity-category attribution groups for the dominant predictors
methods.validity: The intensity partition uses the JTWC categories compared in Figure 6.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.6 Stratified analysis by TC lifecycle and intensity, Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: dominant predictors across JTWC intensity categories
methods.visualObject: Attribution bars separating into JTWC storm-intensity bins
methods.motionPurpose: Expose nonmonotonic predictor sensitivity across storm strength.
methods.stepDetail: Assign each attribution to its JTWC category and compare the dominant predictor magnitudes.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned fitting attribution scoring aggregation and stratification chain recombines on the Typhoon Goni track.

symbols.meaning: Tabular atmospheric, oceanic, and geographic predictor vector
symbols.transition: The visible predictor bars contract into the label x.

symbols.meaning: Predicted tropical-cyclone intensity change over the forecast interval
symbols.transition: The ensemble output marker receives the label f(x).

symbols.unit: model-output units per standardized predictor unit
symbols.transition: Directional probes stack into rows and acquire the label G.

symbols.meaning: Gradient covariance matrix used for global DerSHAP attribution
symbols.transition: The stacked gradient vectors fold into the matrix label C.

symbols.visualObject: Feature-attribution bar formed from one covariance row
symbols.meaning: Global DerSHAP attribution for feature i
symbols.transition: Collapse the selected covariance cells into the label phi_i.

symbols.visualObject: Magnitude ring formed from LAT and LON gradient arrows
symbols.meaning: Main geographic contribution at location x
symbols.transition: The two coordinate arrows close into a norm ring labeled phi_geo(x).

symbols.visualObject: Brightness of the overlap between GEO and feature-j sensitivities
symbols.meaning: Magnitude proxy for the interaction between location and physical feature j
symbols.unit: product of geographic and feature-gradient units
symbols.transition: The overlapping magnitude objects multiply and receive the label I_j(x).

return.visualObject: Typhoon Goni's track over the six model-specific GEO maps, resolving into the XGB POT, shear, and VMPI sensitivity fields along the storm lifecycle
return.claim: Geo-dSHAP identifies where and when ensemble forecasts express physical sensitivity and supports model evaluation beyond accuracy metrics.
return.claimBoundary: The claim is limited to the six tabular ensemble models trained for 24-hour Western North Pacific intensity change; it does not establish behavior for other basins, lead times, or deep-learning architectures.
return.sourceExcerpt: Geo-dSHAP thus provides a diagnostic framework for model evaluation beyond accuracy metrics. By identifying where and when models express physical sensitivity, it delivers physically interpretable insights

visual.primaryObject: Western North Pacific tropical-cyclone track embedded in spatial attribution fields
visual.accentObject: GEO main-effect ring and location-feature interaction hotspots
visual.measuredQuantity: 24-hour intensity change and Geo-dSHAP attribution magnitude
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the basin map and storm track persistent; transform LAT/LON into one GEO object, directional probes into gradients, sparse samples into copula-interpolated fields, and the fields into along-track lifecycle groups.
